passages.explanation: Paul arrives at Derbe and then Lystra, where he meets Timothy — a young disciple whose mother is a Jewish believer and whose father is Greek. Timothy is well regarded by the believers there. Paul wants Timothy to join him, so he circumcises him because of the Jews in the area, who all know his father is Greek. This isn't a contradiction of the Jerusalem Council — Timothy is Jewish through his mother. Paul is being strategic: an uncircumcised half-Jewish companion would be a needless stumbling block in synagogues. As they travel, they deliver the Council's decisions, and the churches are strengthened and grow daily.

Paul and his companions travel through Phrygia and Galatia, but the Holy Spirit prevents them from preaching in the province of Asia. They try to enter Bithynia, but the Spirit of Jesus won't allow it. So they pass through Mysia and go down to Troas, on the Aegean coast. During the night Paul has a vision: a man from Macedonia stands and begs him — come over to Macedonia and help us. They conclude that God is calling them to preach the gospel there. Luke switches to 'we' here, suggesting he joins the group at Troas. The gospel is about to cross into Europe. Sometimes God guides by closing doors, not opening them.

They sail from Troas to Samothrace, then Neapolis, and on to Philippi — a leading city of Macedonia and a Roman colony. On the Sabbath they go outside the city gate to the river, expecting to find a place of prayer. There's no synagogue — you need ten Jewish men to form one. They sit down and speak to the women gathered there. One is Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira, a worshipper of God. The Lord opens her heart to respond to Paul's message. She and her household are baptised, and she insists they stay at her house. The first European convert is a businesswoman. The church at Philippi begins at a riverbank with a handful of women.

A slave girl who has a spirit of divination follows Paul and his companions, shouting: these men are servants of the Most High God who are telling you the way to be saved. She does this for many days until Paul, greatly annoyed, turns and commands the spirit to come out of her in the name of Jesus Christ. It leaves immediately. Her owners, seeing their source of income gone, seize Paul and Silas, drag them to the marketplace, and bring them before the magistrates. The charge is framed politically: these Jews are throwing our city into turmoil, advocating customs unlawful for Romans. The crowd joins the attack. The magistrates order them stripped, beaten with rods, and thrown into the inner cell of the prison with their feet fastened in stocks. The real offence was economic — they destroyed a profitable business.

Around midnight, Paul and Silas are praying and singing hymns — beaten, bleeding, locked in stocks, and worshipping. The other prisoners are listening. Suddenly a violent earthquake shakes the prison. Every door flies open and every chain comes loose. The jailer wakes, sees the doors open, and draws his sword to kill himself — he'd be executed for losing prisoners. Paul shouts: don't harm yourself! We're all here! The jailer rushes in, falls trembling before Paul and Silas, and asks: sirs, what must I do to be saved? Their answer is the gospel in a sentence: believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved — you and your household. They speak the word to everyone in his house. He washes their wounds, and immediately he and his entire household are baptised. He brings them into his house, sets a meal before them, and the whole family is filled with joy — they've come to believe in God. From prison to feast in a single night.

The next morning the magistrates send officers to release Paul and Silas. But Paul refuses to leave quietly: they beat us publicly without a trial, even though we're Roman citizens, and threw us into prison. Now they want to get rid of us secretly? No. Let them come themselves and escort us out. The officers report this, and the magistrates are alarmed — beating a Roman citizen without trial was a serious offence. They come, apologise, escort them out, and ask them to leave the city. Paul and Silas go to Lydia's house, encourage the believers, and then leave. Paul doesn't pull rank for his own comfort — he does it to protect the young church. The magistrates will think twice before harassing believers after this.
